coredata.dc:title: Facile synthesis of magneto-fluorescent carbon dots by one-step microwave-assisted pyrolysis
coredata.dc:creator: Liu, Ruihan
coredata.dc:description: Magneto-fluorescent nanoparticles have been widely designed as multifunctional materials for various advanced applications. Here, a facile approach was established to synthesize magneto-fluorescent carbon dots (MF-CDs) of approximately 2 nm by one-step microwave-assisted pyrolysis of citric acid and ethylenediamine in presence of FeCl2. The FT-IR and XRD analysis revealed the existence of iron oxides in the N-doped CDs. The magnetization analysis demonstrated the mixed phase of Fe3O4 with FeO in the MF-CDs with a saturation magnetization of 0.20 emu/g. The MF-CDs possessed the excitation-independent fluorescence with maximum PL intensity at excitation wavelength of 350 nm. The superparamagnetic and fluorescent behaviors make the designed ultrafine MF-CDs promising potential in multimodal cellular imaging.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Magneto-fluorescent carbon dots
Synthesis
One-step
Microwave-assisted pyrolysis

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM image (A) and DLS results (B) of the MF-CDs.
UV–vis adsorption (A), FT-IR spectrum (B), TGA curve (C), XRD patterns (D) magnetization curve (E) of the MF-CDs.
Fluorescence emission spectra of the proposed MF-CDs (1.0 mg/mL) under different excitation wavelengths (A) and digital photos under daylight and UV light (365 nm) (B).

head.ce:title: Facile synthesis of magneto-fluorescent carbon dots by one-step microwave-assisted pyrolysis

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Materials Physics and Chemistry, School of Materials Science and Engineering, Harbin Institute of Technology, Harbin, 150001, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Materials Physics and Chemistry
School of Materials Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Materials Physics and Chemistry, School of Materials Science and Engineering, Harbin Institute of Technology, Harbin, 150001, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Magneto-fluorescent nanoparticles have been widely designed as multifunctional materials for various advanced applications. Here, a facile approach was established to synthesize magneto-fluorescent carbon dots (MF-CDs) of approximately 2 nm by one-step microwave-assisted pyrolysis of citric acid and ethylenediamine in presence of FeCl2. The FT-IR and XRD analysis revealed the existence of iron oxides in the N-doped CDs. The magnetization analysis demonstrated the mixed phase of Fe3O4 with FeO in the MF-CDs with a saturation magnetization of 0.20 emu/g. The MF-CDs possessed the excitation-independent fluorescence with maximum PL intensity at excitation wavelength of 350 nm. The superparamagnetic and fluorescent behaviors make the designed ultrafine MF-CDs promising potential in multimodal cellular imaging.

ce:section.ce:para: Recently, magneto-fluorescent nanoparticles have been recognized as an emerging class of multifunctional materials with promising potential in biomedical, catalytic, environmental and food analysis fields, by incorporating magnetic nanoparticles and fluorescent dots [1]. Besides the advantages of easy to synthesis and excellent biocompatibility, the superparamagnetic property of Fe3O4 nanoparticles and outstanding superior fluorescence of carbon dots (CDs), they have been widely used in the design and preparation of magneto-fluorescent nanoparticles.

By now, various approaches have been reported for the design of magneto-fluorescent nanoparticles: i) three-step, in which the Fe3O4 nanoparticles and fluorescent CDs were synthesized separately and then the fluorescent CDs were conjugated onto the surface of the Fe3O4 nanoparticles [2–6]; ii) two-step, in which the fluorescent CDs were deposited onto the ready-made Fe3O4 nanoparticles [7,8], in-situ formed in the inner cavity of the hollow NiFe2O4 nanoparticles [9], or the Fe3O4 nanoparticles were synthesized in presence of fluorescent CDs [10–13]; and iii) one-step, by hydrothermal or solvothermal synthesis of both the precursors of Fe3O4 nanoparticles and CDs in one pot as a bottom-up procedure [14]. Comparatively speaking, the one-step method is much simpler, however, toxic ferrocene was used as Fe precursor [14]. There are also some reports which could be classified as pseudo one-step method, in which the Fe precursors (such as ferrocene [15,16] or iron (III) nitrate [17]) were oxidized or reduced before the one-pot hydrothermal or solvothermal treatment at ≥ 200 °C.

The particle size of the reported magneto-fluorescent nanoparticles was mainly in the range of 10–400 nm [7–17]. Unfortunately, the outstanding water dispersion stability of quantum dots could not be achieved due to the big size of such reported magneto-fluorescent nanoparticles. Therefore, there is a great challenge to synthesize the magneto-fluorescent CDs with ultrafine size. To the best of our knowledge, there is no report on the synthesis of magneto-fluorescent CDs by now.

In the present work, a facile one-step approach was established for the synthesis of the magneto-fluorescent CDs (MF-CDs) of approximately 2 nm, by the microwave (MW)-assisted pyrolysis of the most used precursors for the N-doped CDs (citric acid and ethylenediamine) in presence of FeCl2.

Citric acid anhydrous (99.5%) was bought from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. Ethylenediamine (99%) was purchased from Rionlon Bohua (Tianjin) Pharmaceutical Chemical Co. Ltd. Ferrous chloride tetrahydrate (FeCl2·4H2O, 99%) was obtained from Tianjin Chemical Reagent Company. Double distilled water was used throughout.

The MF-CDs were prepared via the microwave method with citric acid and ethylenediamine as raw materials [18], in presence of FeCl2. Typically, 2.0 g of citric acid anhydrous, 1.0 mL of ethylenediamine and 0.624 g of FeCl2·4H2O were dissolved into 20 mL water. The solution was heated in a domestic microwave oven (700 W) for 4 min. After the MW-assisted pyrolysis, black oily residue was obtained. It was dispersed into 40 mL and the MF-CDs were separated from the big particles by centrifugation at 8000 rpm for 10 min. Then the aqueous dispersion of MF-CDs was dialyzed against water (MWCO of 500) for 3 days, replacing water at each 8 h. Finally, the MF-CDs were collected by lyophilization.

Fourier Transform Infrared Spectroscopy (FT-IR) was performed on a Bruker IFS 66 v/s infrared spectrometer, with KBr pellet.
UV–vis spectrum was recorded on a TU-1901 UV–vis spectrometer, with an aqueous dispersion of 0.10 mg/mL.
Thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) was performed on a Perkin-Elmer TGA-7 system at a scan rate of 10 °C/min to 800 °C in N2 atmosphere.
Fluorescent emission spectra were recorded on a Hitachi F-7500 fluorescence spectrometer with aqueous dispersion of 1.0 mg/mL.
The hydrodynamic diameter measurement was performed on a BI-200SM equipment.
The morphology was observed on a JEM1200 EX/S transmission electron microscope (TEM), sampled with aqueous dispersion.
The magnetization M was characterized as a function of the applied magnetic field H in a Lakeshore 7304 magnetosusceptometer.

The magneto-fluorescent CDs (MF-CDs) were synthesized for the first time by the microwave (MW)-assisted pyrolysis of the most used precursors for the N-doped CDs (citric acid and ethylenediamine) in presence of FeCl2. After the MW-assisted pyrolysis, black oily residue was obtained, similar as the N-doped CDs prepared without FeCl2. The phenomenon demonstrated that the addition of FeCl2 did not affect the MW-assisted pyrolysis of citric acid and ethylenediamine. The collected MF-CDs showed near spherical shape with particle size of approximately 2 nm from the TEM analysis (Fig. 1 A). In the DLS analysis, a narrow hydrodynamic diameter distribution was obtained in the range of 3.3–5.3 nm (Fig. 1B). The hydrodynamic diameter from the DLS analysis was bigger than the particle size from the TEM analysis, due to the swelling of the MF-CDs in water.

In the UV–vis spectrum (Fig. 2 A), the product showed two absorption peaks at 240 nm and 340 nm ascribed to the π-π∗ and n-π∗ transition respectively [19], indicating the successful preparation of N-doped CDs in presence of FeCl2. The characteristic absorption of the associative –OH and–NH2 at 3414 cm−1, CO in carboxyl acid group conjugated with condensed aromatic carbon at 1702 cm−1, CO in amide groups at 1645 cm−1, CC stretching of the aromatic carbon at 1563 cm−1, and C–H bending vibration at 1402 cm−1 in the FT-IR spectrum also revealed the successful preparation of N-doped CDs (Fig. 2B) [15,17]. Furthermore, the absorbance corresponding to Fe–O stretching at 575 cm−1 could also be seen, indicating the existence of iron oxide [11,14].

The thermal stability of the proposed MF-CDs was assessed by the TGA technique. Clearly, the initial weight loss at < 100 °C was due to the evaporation of the adsorbed water molecules (Fig. 2C). The following slight weight loss up to 200 °C demonstrated that the MF-CDs were stable up to this temperature. The weight losses of about 40% in the temperature range of 200–500 °C were corresponded to the losses of the plentiful surface oxygen-containing and nitrogen-containing groups [16].

To clarify the species of the iron oxides in the obtained N-CDs, XRD analysis was conducted, as shown in Fig. 2D. Besides the amorphous graphitic carbon (002) in CDs at 23° [11], the diffraction peaks at 43.0° and 62.5° corresponding to the (400) and (440) planes of the standard Fe3O4 (JCPDS01-1111) could be seen [20] respectively, indicating the existence of Fe3O4 in the N-doped CDs. There was no magnetic hysteresis loop in magnetization curve of the MF-CDs, indicating their superparamagnetic property with saturation magnetization of 0.20 emu/g (Fig. 2E). Although the saturation magnetization was lower than the reported Fe3O4 nanoparticles because of their ultrafine size, it was still much higher than the reported carbon nanodots doped super-paramagnetic iron oxide nanoparticles [10], which were successfully used for the multimodal bioimaging and osteochondral tissue regeneration.

The magnetization curve was different from the phase-pure magnetite, maybe due to the mixed phase of Fe3O4 with FeO [21], because only FeCl2 was used as the Fe precursor without any oxidant in the present work. In such case, the oxidation of ferrous ion (Fe(II)) was achieved with oxygen in the air, despite the coexisting citric acid as a possible reductant. To reveal the point, FeCl3 was used as the Fe precursor instead of FeCl2, as a result, non-magnetic N-CDs were obtained by the similar method. The result demonstrated that citric acid could not reduce ferric ion (Fe(III)) in such condition.

Finally, the fluorescent emission behavior of the proposed MF-CDs was investigated with different excitation wavelengths from 330 to 410 nm in aqueous dispersion (1.0 mg/mL). As Shown in Fig. 3 A, the proposed MF-CDs possessed the excitation-independent fluorescence property [22], with maximum PL intensity at excitation wavelength of 350 nm. However, the PL intensity of the proposed MF-CDs was much lower than the reported N-doped CDs which were synthesized without FeCl2. It should be due to the existence of the iron oxides (mixed phase of Fe3O4 with FeO). Even though, the aqueous dispersion of the proposed MF-CDs (0.10 mg/mL) showed strong blue fluorescent intensity under 365 nm UV (Fig. 3B). The features, superparamagnetic and fluorescent behaviors, both of which could be used for bio-imaging, make the proposed MF-CDs promising potential for multimodal cellular imaging.

In summary, magneto-fluorescent carbon dots (MF-CDs) of approximately 2 nm were synthesized by facile one-step microwave (MW)-assisted pyrolysis of the most used precursors for the N-doped CDs (citric acid and ethylenediamine) in presence of FeCl2. The chemical structure, thermal stability, size and morphology of the MF-CDs were characterized with FT-IR, UV–vis, XRD, TGA, TEM and DLS techniques. The superparamagnetic property was revealed by the magnetization analysis and the result showed that the MF-CDs should be hybrid composed with N-CDs and mixed magnetic phase of Fe3O4 with FeO. Additionally, the excitation-independent fluorescence property was also revealed by the fluorescence analysis, exhibiting maximum PL intensity at excitation wavelength of 350 nm. The ultrafine hybrid dots are expected for multimodal cellular imaging owing to their superparamagnetic and fluorescent behaviors.
Ruihan Liu: Conceptualization, Methodology, Investigation, Data curation, Writing - original draft, Writing - review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The author acknowledges Dr. Mingliang Pei, Mr. Pengwei Xie and Mr. Changou Pan with their friendly help of sample analysis works.
